title: A Streetcar Conductor and a Maid's Niece
summary: Interviewed by Joe Mosnier in Oakland at the Black Alliance for Just Immigration in September 2011, Phil Hutchings opens on his family's migration to Cleveland: a father from Macon, Georgia, whose own father went north to drive trackless trolleys, and a mother born outside Memphis who, orphaned young, was raised by an aunt who worked as a maid.

title: The Hatch Act and the Saint James Forum
summary: He describes his lower-middle-class household, his father's steady job as a postal clerk that, under the Hatch Act, barred him from open political activism, and how his father channeled his interests through church forums like the Saint James Forum, where the boy heard local politicians speak.

title: He Never Did a Real Day's Work
summary: Hutchings recalls shaking Senator Robert A. Taft's soft hands at age eight and his Democrat father's retort, his mother going to law school alongside Carl Stokes, who as Cleveland's first Black mayor appointed her chief referee of the Civil Service Commission, and listening to Lowell Thomas and tracing places on a globe.

title: Mother Runs for State Representative
summary: He sketches Cleveland's liberal, ethnically mixed, Irish-machine Democratic politics, his mother's run for state representative on which he campaigned by stuffing her literature into the newspapers he delivered, and a youth civics group the children named the Archegos Club after a Greek word for civic duty.

title: A Small Piece of the East Side
topic: Youth Councils and the Wider World
summary: Hutchings describes growing up confined almost entirely to the African American East Side, the Council on Human Relations youth council that let him meet teenagers across the city, a World Council on Human Affairs mock assembly where his school played France during the Algerian war, and his father teaching him the meaning of a labor union.

title: Born in the Battle of Stalingrad
summary: He recounts the 1958 right-to-work fights in Ohio and California, where Senate and gubernatorial candidates jockeyed and unions flexed their power one last time, his father convincing him never to cross a picket line, and his realization that he was born in 1942 amid Stalingrad and Midway.

title: The Call and Post Inside the Cleveland Press
summary: Hutchings reflects on how Emmett Till frightened him as a teenager, his early childhood summers riding segregated trains down to family near Memphis without understanding it, and reading the Black newspaper the Call and Post, sometimes folded inside the Cleveland Press, as he became aware of the NAACP, Roy Wilkins, and Walter White.

title: Picketing the Dime Stores
summary: He recalls joining the spring 1960 response to the Greensboro sit-ins, picketing the concentrated dime stores along Euclid Avenue with informational signs about their Southern counters, action that alarmed the cautious local NAACP.

title: Two Out of Three
summary: Hutchings describes the NAACP, seeing itself as lawyers and wary of street action that might upset sweetheart deals with Cleveland's white elite, dissolving the youth chapter, the radical ideas he absorbed from the heavily Jewish, Communist-tied Circle Pines camp club, his Democratic-politician mother's three warnings against pregnancy, drug addiction, and communism as she worried over the Fair Play for Cuba group, and reading C. Wright Mills on the power elite.

title: You're Going to Howard
summary: Hutchings tells how, already signed up at Ohio State with a dorm and roommate, his mother phoned to redirect him to Howard University, wanting him among aspiring middle-class Black students, a fraternity, and a future wife, and noting that Howard was actually cheaper than in-state Ohio State.

title: Kennedy Comes to the Chapel
summary: He describes Howard's foreign students from the Caribbean, Africa, and Iran, the charged Kennedy-era Washington atmosphere of activist government and intellectuals, and seeing John F. Kennedy speak in the Howard chapel after a debate with Nixon, then later glimpsing Nixon at a Maryland event.

title: Watching the Soviet Boats
summary: Hutchings recalls living through the Berlin and October 1962 Cuban missile crises in the capital, professors debating whether Washington would be nuked, and his own start as a campus political joiner who ran for office, lost, and managed a freshman-class campaign for a Baltimore student named Nathaniel Knight.

title: Who Are These Folks in Overalls
summary: He explains being drawn to the Nonviolent Action Group students who returned to campus in Southern overalls and one-man-one-vote buttons, people like Stokely Carmichael of the Bronx, Courtland Cox, and Ed Brown, who kept one foot in Howard and spent summers in the South, and who taught him that politics should be about something.

title: Put Your Body on the Line
topic: High School Activists and the Left
summary: Hutchings describes Washington as neutral ground where Southern movement people came up for air, and how high school students challenging segregation laws in Virginia and Maryland, living a life of action rather than preparation, overturned his mother's plan of a slow climb to a profession, then recalls Tom Kahn, a white League for Industrial Democracy activist influenced by Max Shachtman, teaching him the history of the U.S. left as Project Awareness brought controversial speakers like Malcolm X to Howard.
keywords: put your body on the line

title: A Live Communist on Federal Soil
summary: Hutchings tells how Project Awareness also brought the Communist Herbert Aptheker to a Howard nervous about its federal money, and how McCarthy-era fear had silenced brilliant political scientists who could lecture but dared not publish their views, lessons in the repercussions of taking political stands.

title: The White Rice Inn
summary: He recounts a spring 1961 action with CORE under Julius Hobson, sent with a racially mixed group to a Chinese-owned Baltimore restaurant called the White Rice Inn, where the proprietor, calling himself a colored man too, pleaded with them to leave and offered a free meal at his other restaurant.

title: That's What I Mean by Freedom
summary: Hutchings describes the group leaving, deciding they had punked out, and then returning to sit on the restaurant steps and link arms until the police pried them apart and jailed them, a moment of liberation when he realized he was making a stand for the life he wanted and crossing a Rubicon.

title: Like a Thirteenth Grade
summary: He gives his disappointment with Howard as a colonial-feeling thirteenth grade anxious about its federal masters, naming a Democratic-active political science teacher named Martin and a French-resistance veteran professor who brought first-class thinking, against a boring Negro History course of dates, and recalls the campus on the day Kennedy was killed in November 1963.

title: We Didn't Think About Gender
summary: Hutchings reflects on the active women of NAG, naming Jeannie Bell, Bill Mahoney, Jean Wheeler who married Frank Smith, and Ruth Howard, and explains how NAG's repeated failure to win campus recognition turned into a strength, freeing the group to meet off campus and connect with students across Washington's universities.

title: Records Out of the State
summary: Hutchings recounts a summer trip back to Tennessee, probably 1962, to see cousins near Memphis where he finally registered the segregation signs and the harassment of voter-registration efforts, then his particular task in Mississippi in 1964: duplicating Mississippi Freedom Democratic Party voter records and getting them out of the state, moving among second-district projects including Ruleville where Fannie Lou Hamer was, valuing most the grassroots democracy of community meetings.

title: A Song That Read the Room
summary: He explains the organizers' lesson of speaking complicated ideas in people's own language without dumbing them down, and watching Fannie Lou Hamer break into a song that reflected the mood of the room, a profound learning about culture used in a political way, while distancing himself from church and faith.

title: Mississippi, God Damn
summary: Hutchings likens Mississippi to a Vietnam-style pressure cooker, citing his project-house friends Wazir Willie Peacock and Hollis Watkins, where activists faced snipers and hostile officials, the FBI watching but doing nothing, and the constant possibility of being killed.

title: Paul and Silas, Bound in Jail
summary: He reflects that ninety percent of what he did in the movement he could only do because others did it with him, the church as Southern sanctuary, prisoners singing to drive jailers crazy and create community in jail, and getting tear-gassed at a 1964 Cambridge, Maryland march when George Wallace came to town.

title: A Depression Settled on the Movement
summary: Hutchings describes the depression that settled over the movement when the MFDP was not seated at Atlantic City while dealing with Lyndon Johnson, a delayed shutdown reaction he likens to mourning his mother in 1991, and going north to work in Robert Kennedy's 1964 Senate campaign in the research and speakers' bureau, distinguishing a liberal Democrat from a liberal Republican, on the rationale that Kennedy understood the movement's experience and could grow.

title: Both SNCC and SDS
summary: Hutchings explains his unusual position in both SNCC and SDS, meeting Tom Hayden, Al Haber, and Richard Flacks through an old Circle Pines friend, David Strauss, at the University of Michigan, and learning of SDS's Economic Research and Action Project to build an interracial movement of the poor in Northern cities.

title: Turn Birmingham Into Chicago
summary: He recalls Tom Hayden recruiting him over drinks to come see the Newark project, the appeal of a statewide New Jersey movement linked by the turnpike, and his conviction that defeating Jim Crow would only place Black people in the real economic and political problems facing the whole country, so the North had to be organized.

title: Housing, Rent, and Police
summary: Hutchings describes arriving to find no interracial movement of the poor and no white people to organize, doing unemployment surveys but finding that residents wanted to work on housing, rent, and police brutality, breaking with the liberal Clinton Hill association, and watching the 1965 Watts rebellion on television with Tom Hayden.

title: A Colonial African Nation
summary: He recounts developing a two-year game plan after the Watts conversation, and how, as the movement turned more racially conscious, the Newark organizers framed a predominantly Black city against an Italian power structure as a colonial African nation, arguing for Black institutions, community control, and even Black policemen so people could see the system at work through Black agents.

title: Real Grassroots Power
summary: Hutchings describes having real grassroots power that made the mayor and city council leery, the Negro machine politicians who broke their windows and slashed tires, a near-altercation on Springfield Avenue with the local Democratic boss, and funding that came mostly from labor unions like the UAW and the Episcopal church, the latter connecting him to Nathan Wright Jr.

title: The Limits of White Organizing
summary: He explains how by 1965 the white SDS organizers of the NCUP project, recognizing the limits of white organizing in a Black community with no interracial movement materializing, shifted to fundraising and research on city structures, while he answered LeRoi Jones's pointed question about whom he represented by pointing to the ninety-nine-percent Black Central Ward.

title: Let's Become the Poverty Program
topic: Taking Over the War on Poverty
summary: Hutchings recounts debating whether to ignore or capture the federal poverty program, a strategy seeded by an earlier United Freedom Party third-party gambit, and deciding to seize it, installing their own people like Jesse Allen and chairperson Bessie Smith on the boards and effectively controlling the two poorest Newark districts.

title: A Welcome Guest at Dinner
summary: Hutchings reflects on how the poverty program's push-button phones and leather couches drew organizers off the streets and eroded the community ownership that storefront offices with donated furniture had built, how the community nominated him for a twelve-thousand-dollar community-action-director post the top officials denied him, and how he could never account economically for surviving the 1960s, with a planned system of dropping by households at dinner time bringing neighborhood news.

title: The Arch Bad Guy
summary: He recounts making the mayor's chief of staff Donald Malafronte his public villain, a wealthy white supporter named Derek Wynans who started a community newspaper called the Newark Advance for which Hutchings wrote a column, and a sympathetic Star-Ledger reporter, all signs the organizers had become a force noticed well beyond the neighborhood.

title: The March to Name the System
summary: Hutchings describes being released from Newark to organize, with SDS lead organizer Paul Booth, the April 1965 march in Washington that became the first major protest against the Vietnam War, drawing on his earlier 1963 experience helping Bayard Rustin's March on Washington committee mobilize the people of D.C.

title: A Gradual Shift
summary: He recounts organizing D.C. churches and community groups for the 1965 march alongside Norman Hill of CORE, then returning to Newark, and reflects on the gradual shift as SDS people moved toward the antiwar movement while Tom Hayden, after traveling to Vietnam, returned to humanize the Vietnamese, before Stokely Carmichael came to Newark inviting Hutchings into SNCC's new direction.

title: The True Bearers of Change
summary: Hutchings lays out his philosophy, formed through SNCC and the Newark SDS experience, that the true bearers of future change were the grassroots poor with the least stake in the existing order, building a prototype of the future society with allies in the church, unions, and universities, just as Carmichael, in spring 1966, prepared to replace John Lewis as SNCC chair.

title: Come On In at the Bottom
topic: The Northern Machine and the Press
summary: Hutchings explains that the Southern movement had fought to get into a Democratic Party that kept it out, but when Daley welcomed King into Chicago at the bottom of the machine, neither had an answer to racism without segregation laws, and describes the United Freedom Party alliance with former assemblyman George Richardson as beyond the door-knockers' scope, the movement's lack of its own institutions forcing reliance on a white press that framed Stokely Carmichael as a volatile wild man.

title: A Sense of Black Manhood
summary: He characterizes his bond with Stokely Carmichael as both personal and political, knowing his family and once briefly courting his sister, a relationship of mutual challenge and admiration, and recounts traveling with Carmichael on the speaking trips that earned SNCC badly needed thousand-dollar fees as the Newark rebellion of 1967 approached.

title: You Got Back Just in Time to Start the Riot
summary: Hutchings describes debriefing Carmichael on messaging during plane trips with Cleveland Sellers and Charles Lloyd, his father's wry remark that he had returned just in time to start the riot, and the widening gulf with a father who thought his son, the first in the family to nearly finish college, was wasting his life.

title: All Hell Broke Loose
summary: He recounts the night the Newark rebellion began: returning from visiting a VISTA volunteer past midnight, hearing sirens and gunshots, and learning that all hell had broken loose at the police station a block from his home where a taxi driver had been beaten and held inside while a crowd threw rocks, with his neighbor and guide BJ at the scene.

title: Only What Is White-Owned
topic: In the Streets of the Rebellion
summary: Hutchings describes how he and BJ, who knew everyone in the Central Ward, agreed not to stop the uprising but to direct it only against white-owned property, distinguishing their clear intent from Detroit SNCC, which told people off the streets, and recounts cockily yelling black power near the Hayes Homes projects, being stopped by eight policemen with a bartender named Joe Whitley, and being beaten with rubber truncheons that left no marks, his nonviolent training letting him absorb the blows.

title: A Rebellion, Not a Riot
summary: Hutchings tells of being caught out past curfew, taken in by residents who knew the Newark group's work while snipers fired at police, and overhearing an older Black woman say it was about time the rebellion came here, a sign their racial organizing had taken effect, and insists on calling it a rebellion rather than a riot so participants would feel unapologetic, emerging more inspired by the community's self-assertion than discouraged by the state's police response.

title: A Black Mayor for Newark
summary: Hutchings describes turning the rebellion toward Black political power, a 1966 mock mayoral campaign modeled on the MFDP to get people imagining a Black mayor, settling on civil engineer Kenneth Gibson, and the 1970 Black and Puerto Rican convention, with Hilda Hidalgo's support, that nominated Gibson, who won when four Italians split the vote.

title: Newark Is Our Battleground
summary: He recounts working with Nathan Wright Jr. to organize the first National Conference on Black Power after the rebellion, uniting progressive Black politicians, businesspeople, and nationalists amid the era's Motown and Aretha Franklin soundtrack, with LeRoi Jones framing a modern nationalism rooted in Newark rather than Africa and bringing in the Nation of Islam toward a Black United Front.

title: Shooting Ourselves in the Foot
topic: The Cost of Opposing the War
summary: Hutchings reflects on the financial and political repercussions SNCC suffered, like King's SCLC, for opposing the Vietnam War, since civil rights was seen as a domestic Black matter and stepping into foreign policy lost mainstream supporters who had backed the Mississippi challenge, the freedom party, and the freedom schools.

title: The Paper Sold Out in Five Minutes
summary: He recounts SNCC researcher Jack Minnis's 1966 paper that led the organization to come out against Zionism and Israel as an oppressor of the Palestinians, a stir in liberal circles, and being physically mobbed at a Manhattan meeting by people seizing every copy of the SNCC newspaper carrying the article.

title: Agents in the Movement
summary: Hutchings describes the liberalism-versus-radicalism tensions over Israel and Vietnam, SNCC's support for the National Liberation Front, and the dawning awareness of COINTELPRO and FBI efforts to divide the Black movement and pit the Panthers against SNCC, including a New Jersey leader offered a hundred dollars on his bed by agents.

title: Alliance or Merger
summary: Hutchings recounts becoming SNCC chairman as the Panthers drafted Stokely Carmichael as Prime Minister, James Forman as Minister of Foreign Affairs, and Rap Brown as Minister of Justice in 1967, celebrated at Huey Newton's birthday party in Oakland, amid tensions over whether SNCC's intellectuals should become Panthers, and how he voted the Panther program good but not SNCC's own, declaring as chairman there would be no merger and not even a clear alliance before turning to local groups like the Black Liberators of St. Louis.

title: Treated Like a Mafia Don
summary: He tells of getting arrested with the Black Liberators under an old anti-labor unlawful-assembly law, his lawyer uncle joining William Kunstler on the defense and playing his speech to the jury, who acquitted him, and being treated as a celebrity in the workhouse of the Black town of Kinloch, where the warden let them use the phone for the press.

title: On a Downward Cycle Since Atlantic City
summary: Hutchings reflects on taking SNCC's helm amid declining membership and money, dispirited meetings, and the divisive expulsion of white members, his belief he could turn it around in a different form, and the core difference from the Panthers' chapter model: SNCC always built people's own organizations like the Lowndes County movement and the MFDP, knowing the organizers would leave.

title: A National Consortium of the Grassroots
summary: He describes his focus on joining radical grassroots groups around the country (the Black Liberators in St. Louis, JOMO in Florida, Watts-rooted groups in Los Angeles) into a national consortium empowered by national networks, contrasting it with Carmichael's Black United Front idea, which he doubted could hold disparate interests together against the white power elite.

title: Somewhere Closer That Needs Your Help
summary: Hutchings recounts how the Venceremos Brigade arose when the Vietnamese, inspired by the Abraham Lincoln Brigade idea, redirected American radicals' wish to fight in Vietnam toward helping Cuba instead, and how the movements organized the first brigades, the first drawing about two hundred mostly white volunteers against a projected nine hundred, the second over eight hundred in March 1970.

title: I Can't Dance in My Revolution
summary: Hutchings reflects on how the brigade let him view the U.S. movement objectively and meet people from across the country, his identification with Cubans who cut cane all day then danced and drank rum, a debate with Grenadians who said their liberation needed more space for God, and the lesson in discipline when the Cubans synchronized watches for a six o'clock meeting, a corrective to the movement's casual CP time.

title: Can You Talk About Your Country
summary: He recalls Cuba as a center for revolutionaries worldwide, meeting an Angolan named Ernesto and Brazilians and Vietnamese who could analyze their whole countries, and a Vietnamese man who pressed him for two hours to dissect the U.S. labor movement past his stereotype of sellouts, a learning process about serious conversation grounded in real things.

title: Wondering What Halibut Tastes Like
summary: Hutchings tells of staying on at the Havana Libre, the former Hilton, learning to like fresh fish that Japanese-introduced refrigeration had spread across the island, helping plan with the Yippies and the Vietnamese an antiwar conference held in Cuba for its access, and a young Vietnamese man who after intensive U.S. bombing said he was too anti-American to meet that day but it was not personal, amid the easy affection and liberated humor among the Vietnamese.

title: The Government Is the Problem
topic: A Reversed Logic, a Continuing Question
summary: Hutchings contrasts the sixties logic of forcing the federal government in against racist state officials with his present immigration work, where the government is the problem, since a world system of corporations and laws like NAFTA drives migration the U.S. press discusses only at the receiving end, and closes that the work continues to refine concepts around how race, class, and increasingly gender come together, a question he began in the sixties and carries into the twenty-first century.
